blocks.title: Hidden Routes in First-Passage Time
blocks.spoken_text: In recent months, I have been developing a research line around one question: if a random walker can reach a target through more than one effective pathway, what can the hitting-time distribution tell us about those hidden routes? I am especially interested in distributions that do not stay single-peaked, but develop an early peak, a valley, and a later peak. My goal today is to show that this shape is not just visual texture. It is a mechanism clue. I will start from a very simple single-target ring, then move to a two-dimensional geometry, and finally explain why the next natural step is to move from one target to many potential targets.
blocks.timing_prompt: Open with the scientific question and the three-step roadmap: basic idea, single-target mechanism, many-target outlook.

blocks.title: What Is First-Passage Time?
blocks.spoken_text: Let me first define the quantity, because not everyone here works on random walks. A random walk is simply a process that moves step by step with some stochastic rule. The first-passage time asks a very specific question: when does the walker hit the target for the first time? We then repeat the experiment many times and collect those first-hit times into a distribution. This is important because the distribution keeps information that one average value would erase. If most trajectories arrive through one characteristic route, we often see one main timescale. But if the system supports different ways of arriving, with different delays, then the full distribution can separate those possibilities. So the object I study is simple to define, but surprisingly rich as a mechanism diagnostic.
blocks.timing_prompt: Teach the quantity from zero background. Point to the animation: one path, one first hit, then repeated trials.

blocks.title: Why a Double Peak Matters
blocks.spoken_text: Once we have the distribution, the next question is why its shape matters. A single peak usually means one dominant arrival scale, even if there is still noise around it. A double peak is more interesting. It suggests that the process is not organized by one timescale alone. Instead, there are at least two characteristic ways of reaching the target, or two classes of trajectories with visibly different delays. The valley between them is also informative. It means there is an intermediate time window where neither branch is dominating the statistics. So already at a qualitative level, the distribution is telling us something structural about the search process. This is the intuition that motivates the rest of the talk: the shape is not just output; it is compressed mechanism information.
blocks.timing_prompt: Keep this intuitive. The audience only needs to buy the idea that shape can encode route competition.

blocks.title: Single Target on a Ring
blocks.spoken_text: I begin with the simplest single-target model because it isolates the mechanism cleanly. On a ring, the walker starts at one site and tries to hit one target. The left panel shows a case that stays effectively single-peaked: there is still some shortcut structure, but it does not separate enough from the rest of the circulation. The right panel shows the case I care about: a visible fast branch and a surviving delayed branch. So the lesson is not that every shortcut creates a bimodal distribution. The lesson is that a bimodal distribution appears only when two route families remain both distinct in time and significant in weight. That is why the ring is useful. It gives the cleanest minimal picture of when one target produces one peak and when one target produces two.
blocks.timing_prompt: This is the first mechanism page. Use the ring to make fast branch and slow detour feel obvious.

blocks.title: Route Design Changes the Distribution
blocks.spoken_text: The next step in the recent work was to ask what actually controls the second peak. The answer is more specific than simply saying that a shortcut exists. The way the shortcut is configured matters. As the destination is retuned, the late branch can grow, become clearest in one intermediate regime, and then weaken again. So the effect is not monotone. This matters because it means route design is part of the physics. The distribution is not responding to a vague topological change. It is responding to a concrete change in branch timing and branch weight. That is the real single-target result I want to emphasize here: not every shortcut creates the same story, and small route edits can move the system between single-peak-like and clearly bimodal behavior.
blocks.timing_prompt: This is your recent single-target contribution page. Emphasize configuration, not just existence.

blocks.title: From a Ring to a 2D Geometry
blocks.spoken_text: After that, I asked which kinds of spatial configuration can actually create a double peak in two dimensions. The answer is that geometry has to support two route families that are both viable. In the one-target corridor picture, that means a direct corridor branch and a delayed outside-and-return branch must both survive. In the two-target picture, geometry can add another mechanism on top: the walker may not only choose a route, but also effectively choose which target wins. This matters because it shows that the ring story was not a toy artifact. It was the minimal version of a mechanism that survives in spatial models, but now geometry decides whether the two timescales remain visible or collapse back into one broad mode.
blocks.timing_prompt: Sell the transfer: the same logic survives, but now it becomes physically and geometrically interpretable.

blocks.title: Valley Is a Budget Structure
blocks.spoken_text: The next question is whether the late branch can be explained simply by membrane passage. The permeability scan says no. As permeability increases, the membrane-related share does rise, but it levels off at roughly fifty percent instead of taking over the whole late branch. So membrane traffic clearly matters, but it does not by itself define peak two. That is the main point of this slide. A membrane-only explanation would keep pushing the late branch toward complete dominance. The fact that the scan saturates means the rest of the delayed structure must still depend on the broader corridor-plus-outside geometry, not on membrane passage alone.
blocks.timing_prompt: Slow down here. This is the deepest slide, and the audience only needs one key idea: valley is a budget structure.

blocks.title: From One Target to Many Potential Targets
blocks.spoken_text: I want to end with the direction that now feels most natural. So far, most of the mechanism language has been built in single-target systems. But once there are two targets, a peak may correspond not just to a route family, but to a route-target pair. And once there are many targets, target density and target classes may introduce entirely new late-time winners. So the next open question is not simply whether the distribution is bimodal. It is what physical mechanism is making it bimodal. Is it route delay, target choice, screening by nearby targets, or some mixture of all three? For me, that is the natural next step of this research line: move from hidden routes around one target to hidden route-and-target competition in multi-target systems.
blocks.timing_prompt: End with momentum. Do not add extra future directions; keep the finish on many potential targets.
